title: Discrete Mathematics
description: Logic, sets, counting, and graphs — foundations of algorithms and CS.

questions.explanation:
De Morgan's law: $
eg(P \land Q) \equiv (
eg P) \lor (
eg Q)$.

Negating an AND flips it to an OR of negations.

questions.prompt: An implication $P \Rightarrow Q$ is false only when:
questions.explanation:
$P \Rightarrow Q$ fails only when a true premise leads to a false conclusion.

If $P$ is false, the implication is considered true (vacuous truth).

Intersection contains elements in both sets: $2$ and $3$.

So $A \cap B = \{2, 3\}$.

questions.prompt: If $|A| = 5$ and $|B| = 3$ and $A \cap B = \emptyset$, then $|A \cup B| =$
questions.explanation:
For disjoint sets, $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| = 5 + 3 = 8$.

In general $|A \cup B| = |A| + |B| - |A \cap B|$.

questions.prompt: How many subsets does a set with $n$ elements have?
questions.explanation:
Each element may be in or out of a subset: $2$ choices per element.

Total subsets: $2^n$ (including $\emptyset$ and the set itself).

questions.prompt: The number of ways to choose $2$ items from $5$ (order irrelevant) is $C(5,2) =$
questions.explanation:
$$C(n,k) = \dfrac{n!}{k!(n-k)!} = \dfrac{5!}{2! \, 3!} = \dfrac{120}{2 \cdot 6} = 10.$$

Combinations ignore order; permutations do not.

$$P(n,k) = \dfrac{n!}{(n-k)!} = 5 \times 4 = 20.$$

Order matters: first choice $5$ options, second $4$.

questions.prompt: In a simple undirected graph, the handshaking lemma says the sum of degrees is:
choices: Equal to the number of vertices
Equal to twice the number of edges
Equal to the number of edges
questions.explanation:
Each edge contributes $1$ to the degree of two endpoints.

So $\sum_v \deg(v) = 2|E|$.

questions.prompt: A tree with $n$ vertices has how many edges?
questions.explanation:
A tree is a connected acyclic graph.

Any tree on $n$ vertices has exactly $n - 1$ edges.

$f = O(g)$ means $f$ grows no faster than a constant times $g$ (asymptotically).

Since $n \leq n^2$ for $n \geq 1$, we have $n = O(n^2)$. The reverse $n^2 = O(n)$ is false.

questions.prompt: Binary search on a sorted array of size $n$ has typical time complexity:
questions.explanation:
Each step halves the search space.

Number of halvings until one element remains is about $\log_2 n$, so $O(\log n)$.

questions.prompt: Proof by induction requires a base case and:
choices: Assuming the statement for $k$ and proving it for $k+1$
questions.explanation:
Mathematical induction: prove $P(1)$ (or $P(0)$), then show $P(k) \Rightarrow P(k+1)$.

This establishes $P(n)$ for all $n$ in the intended range.

questions.prompt: A function $f: A \to B$ is injective (one-to-one) if:
questions.explanation:
Injective means distinct inputs map to distinct outputs.

Surjective (onto) means every element of $B$ is an image.

Law of excluded middle: $P$ or not $P$ is always true.

A tautology is true under every truth assignment.

questions.prompt: BFS (breadth-first search) on an unweighted graph finds:
choices: Shortest paths in number of edges from the source
questions.explanation:
BFS explores level by level.

In an unweighted graph, the first time a node is reached is via a minimum-edge path from the source.
